app.title: Compensated Thresholds
app.subtitle: Aerobic threshold analysis for trail running

single.title: Single activity
single.desc: Analyze one run and calculate drift

multi.title: Multiple activities
multi.desc: Load several runs and find threshold automatically

history.title: History
history.desc: See aerobic threshold development over time

how.title: How does this work?
how.single: Load one FIT file — the app calculates GAP-compensated drift and DFA alpha1 analysis (if HRV data available)
how.multi: Load 3–6 runs at different HR zones → automatic threshold detection
how.history: Save results and track AeT development throughout training
how.methods: HR drift (Pa:Hr decoupling) and DFA alpha1 (HRV-based). When both are available, they cross-validate each other.

noHrv.title: DFA Alpha1 not available
noHrv.text: The file contains no RR interval data. DFA alpha1 analysis requires a chest strap heart rate monitor (Polar H10, Garmin HRM-Pro) that records beat-to-beat data. Enable HRV recording on your device.

app.tooFewPoints: Too few data points in selected range. Extend the analyzed section.
app.footer: Based on <uphill>Uphill Athlete</uphill> method, <tp>TrainingPeaks</tp> Pa:Hr analysis, and <rogers>Rogers et al. (2021)</rogers> DFA alpha1 research.

upload.title: Upload FIT file
upload.instruction: Drag and drop a .FIT file here or click to select
upload.hint: Export from Garmin Connect: Activity → ⚙️ → Export original

zoom.tightTitle: Tight: analyzed section HR range only

drift.heading: Drift analysis

below.desc: Drift {{value}}% is below 3.5%. Your aerobic threshold is likely higher. Repeat the test 5 bpm higher.

at.desc: Drift {{value}}% is between 3.5–5%. Your aerobic threshold is approximately {{hr}} bpm.

above.desc: Drift {{value}}% is above 5%. Starting heart rate was too high. Repeat the test at a lower heart rate.

drift.intro: The analyzed section is split into two equal halves by time. Efficiency Factor (EF = GAP speed / heart rate) is compared between halves: a drop in EF means heart rate has "drifted" upward — a sign you are running at or above your aerobic threshold.

note.explanation: EF (Efficiency Factor) = speed / heart rate. When EF drops in the second half, heart rate has "drifted" upward relative to pace. The GAP-compensated value accounts for terrain, normalizing the effect of hills.

noData.hint: DFA alpha1 requires beat-to-beat RR interval data. Make sure you're using a chest strap (e.g. Polar H10, Garmin HRM-Pro) with HRV recording enabled.

hrv1.label: HRVT1 (AeT)
hrv1.desc: Your aerobic threshold. Below this heart rate your body runs primarily on fat and aerobic metabolism. This is the upper limit for Zone 2 and long easy runs.

hrv2.label: HRVT2 (AnT/LT2)
hrv2.desc: Your lactate threshold (LT2 / VT2). Above this heart rate, lactate accumulates faster than it clears. Use for tempo and threshold interval targets — sustainable only for ~40–60 min at most.

crossVal.label: Cross-validation
crossVal.desc: Compares the AeT from drift analysis with HRVT1 from DFA. Both methods target the same physiological boundary. Close agreement validates the result.

chart.vsTitle: DFA Alpha1 vs. heart rate — thresholds

sens.summaryRobust: Result is robust — small parameter changes have no significant effect.
sens.summaryStable: Result is reasonably stable, but some parameters do affect it.
sens.summarySensitive: Result is sensitive to parameter changes — interpret with caution.
sens.summaryGapModel: GAP model difference: {{diff}}%-units — terrain significantly affects the result.
sens.summaryCIWide: Confidence interval is wide ({{lower}}–{{upper}}%) — dataset is noisy.
sens.summaryWarmup: Warmup cutoff significantly affects the result — verify the correct cutoff point.
sens.summaryTooFewData: Sensitivity analysis could not run — too little data.
sens.warmupNote: How much drift changes with different warmup cutoff points. Stable chart = robust result.

gapNote.significant: Model difference: {{diff}}% units. Terrain significantly affects the result.
gapNote.minor: Model difference: {{diff}}% units. Minor difference — terrain has little effect.

sens.splitNote: How much drift changes when moving the half-split point. 50% = standard equal split.

ci.tooFew: Not enough data for bootstrap analysis.
ci.warningAeT: ⚠ Confidence interval crosses the 3.5% threshold — AeT estimate is uncertain.
ci.warningLT: ⚠ Confidence interval crosses the 5% threshold — interpretation may vary.

empty.title: No saved analyses yet
empty.text: Analyze an activity in single or multi-file view and save the result using the 'Save to history' button. Over time you'll see how your aerobic threshold develops.

multi.heading: Multi-activity analysis
multi.desc: Load multiple runs at different HR zones. The app finds at which heart rate drift exceeds 3.5% and estimates your aerobic threshold.

threshold.label: Estimated aerobic threshold (AeT)
threshold.hint: Add more activities near the threshold zone for a better estimate.

desc.atThreshold: {{count}} activities landed in the 3.5–5% drift zone. Aerobic threshold is approximately {{hr}} bpm.
desc.interpolated: Interpolated from activities: {{lowHR}} bpm ({{lowDrift}}% drift) and {{highHR}} bpm ({{highDrift}}% drift). HR gap: {{gap}} bpm.
desc.allBelow: All activities were below 3.5% drift. AeT is likely above {{hr}} bpm. Test at a higher heart rate.
desc.allAbove: All activities exceeded 5% drift. AeT is likely below {{hr}} bpm. Test at a lower heart rate.

chart.title: HR vs. Drift — Aerobic threshold detection

how.title: How does multi-activity analysis work?
how.step1: Load 3–6 runs at different intensities (easy, moderate, brisk)
how.step2: The app calculates GAP-compensated drift for each
how.step3: The chart shows drift relative to heart rate
how.step4: The app interpolates at which HR the 3.5% threshold is crossed = AeT
how.note: Best results when activities are steady-state (no intervals), at least 40 min long, and at different HR zones.

hrCompleteness.name: HR data completeness
hrCompleteness.detail: {{percent}}% of data points contain HR data ({{count}}/{{total}})

hrPlausibility.name: HR plausibility
hrPlausibility.pass: All HR values are physiologically valid (30–220 bpm)

gpsAlt.name: GPS altitude quality
gpsAlt.fail: {{count}} large altitude jump(s) (> 10 m) — GPS noise

sampling.name: Sampling rate
sampling.detail: Average interval: {{interval}} s, {{gaps}} gap(s) in data

issue.hrMissing: HR data missing for {{percent}}% of data points
issue.hrSpikes: {{count}} HR spike(s) — possible chest strap contact issue
issue.gpsNoise: Significant GPS altitude noise — GAP calculation accuracy may be affected
issue.dataGaps: Multiple data gaps — watch auto-pause or GPS dropout
issue.terrainImbalance: Half terrain profiles differ significantly — drift result may be skewed

guide.heading: How to read these results
guide.overview: This analysis detected your aerobic threshold (AeT) — the heart rate where your aerobic system shifts to a more mixed energy supply. Two independent methods cross-validate each other for higher confidence.

drift.title: Drift analysis (Pa:Hr decoupling)
drift.text: Compares the first and second half of the run. When heart rate rises relative to pace (EF drops), your body works harder to hold the same speed — a sign you are at or above AeT. GAP compensation removes the effect of hills so the test is valid on any terrain.

dfa.title: DFA Alpha1 (HRV-based)
dfa.text: Analyzes beat-to-beat heart rate variability in rolling 120-second windows. As intensity rises, the variability pattern changes. α1 = 0.75 marks the aerobic threshold (HRVT1 = AeT); α1 = 0.50 marks the anaerobic threshold (HRVT2 = LT2). Requires a chest strap with RR interval recording (e.g. Polar H10, Garmin HRM-Pro).

crossval.title: Cross-validation (inside DFA panel)
crossval.text: Compares the AeT from drift analysis with HRVT1 from DFA. Both target the same physiological threshold — close agreement confirms the result. A large difference may mean the test effort was at the wrong intensity or that one dataset had quality issues.

diagnostics.title: Analysis reliability
diagnostics.text: Scores data quality (GPS, HR sensor, sampling rate) and result robustness (sensitivity to warmup cutoff and split point). Check this if the drift or DFA results seem unexpected — low scores indicate the test conditions were not ideal.

reading.heading: Color coding in drift analysis
reading.below: Green — Below AeT. Effort was easy; your true threshold is higher.
reading.at: Yellow — At AeT. Threshold found. This is your Zone 2 ceiling.
reading.above: Red — Above AeT. Effort was too intense; threshold is lower.

guide.linked: Drift AeT and DFA HRVT1 both estimate the same threshold from different angles. When they agree, save the result to History to track how your aerobic fitness develops over training.
